---
title: "Fraud Detection Analysis"
description: "Statistical and ML-based fraud detection using Logistic Regression and Random Forest on 500 financial transactions"
author: "Rohith Krishna Thazhathethil Chandraprakash"
date: "2025-03-01"
categories: [Machine Learning, Fraud Detection, Python, Statistics]
---
::: {.close-button-container}
<a href="../index.html" class="close-button" title="Close and return to Projects">
  <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round">
    <line x1="18" y1="6" x2="6" y2="18"></line>
    <line x1="6" y1="6" x2="18" y2="18"></line>
  </svg>
</a>
:::
## Overview

This individual project examines a fraud detection dataset to identify
patterns distinguishing fraudulent from genuine transactions. Using
statistical methods and predictive modelling, it delivers a fraud
mitigation strategy for upper management.

::: {.callout-note}
## Dataset
500 transactions, 23 variables. Target: `is_fraud`.
Nearly balanced — 246 fraud cases (49.2%) vs 254 legitimate (50.8%).
:::

## Analysis Pipeline

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[500 Transactions] --> B[Data Cleaning]
  B --> C[IQR Outlier Capping]
  C --> D[One-Hot Encoding]
  D --> E[75/25 Train-Test Split]
  E --> F[Z-Score & IQR Flagging]
  E --> G[Logistic Regression]
  E --> H[Random Forest 200 trees]
  F --> I[Insights]
  G --> I
  H --> I
```

## Fraud vs Non-Fraud Distribution

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "The dataset is balanced with 49.2% fraud and 50.8% legitimate transactions"

import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Transaction Type': ['Non-Fraud (Class 0)', 'Fraud (Class 1)'],
    'Count': [254, 246]
})

fig = px.bar(df, x='Transaction Type', y='Count',
             title='Fraud vs Non-Fraud Distribution',
             color='Transaction Type',
             color_discrete_map={
                 'Non-Fraud (Class 0)': '#2980B9',
                 'Fraud (Class 1)': '#E74C3C'
             })
fig.update_layout(template='plotly_white', showlegend=False)
fig.show()
```

## Model Performance

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Random Forest outperforms Logistic Regression across all metrics"

import plotly.graph_objects as go

metrics = ['Accuracy', 'Fraud Recall', 'Fraud Precision', 'ROC-AUC']
logistic = [0.68, 0.74, 0.65, 0.675]
random_forest = [0.70, 0.75, 0.68, 0.692]

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    name='Logistic Regression',
    x=metrics, y=logistic,
    marker_color='#2980B9'
))
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(
    name='Random Forest',
    x=metrics, y=random_forest,
    marker_color='#8E44AD'
))

fig.update_layout(
    barmode='group',
    title='Logistic Regression vs Random Forest',
    yaxis=dict(range=[0.5, 0.85], title='Score'),
    template='plotly_white',
    legend_title='Model'
)
fig.show()
```

## Key Statistical Findings

```{python}
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Comparison of key demographic and financial features between fraud and non-fraud groups"

import plotly.graph_objects as go

categories = ['Avg Age', 'Personal Income (k)', 'Credit Score', 'Avg Transaction ($)']
fraud_vals =     [51.7, 56.2, 645, 1850]
non_fraud_vals = [48.6, 64.1, 651, 2100]

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='Fraud',     x=categories, y=fraud_vals,     marker_color='#E74C3C'))
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='Non-Fraud', x=categories, y=non_fraud_vals, marker_color='#2ECC71'))

fig.update_layout(
    barmode='group',
    title='Fraud vs Non-Fraud: Key Feature Comparison',
    template='plotly_white',
    legend_title='Class'
)
fig.show()
```

## Model Comparison Table

| Metric | Logistic Regression | Random Forest |
|--------|--------------------:|:-------------:|
| Accuracy | 0.68 | **0.70** |
| Fraud Recall | 0.74 | **0.75** |
| Fraud Precision | 0.65 | **0.68** |
| ROC-AUC | 0.675 | **0.692** |

::: {.callout-tip}
## Conclusion
Random Forest provides superior performance and is the preferred model
for operational fraud detection. Fraud does not always appear in
unusually large transactions — combining ML with statistical flagging
(Z-score + IQR) gives the most robust detection approach.
:::

## Recommendations

- Use Random Forest as the primary detection model
- Implement real-time transaction risk scoring using model probability outputs
- Prioritise awareness campaigns for older and lower-income customer segments
- Combine Z-score and IQR anomaly checks with ML predictions for a layered defence

::: {.callout-note}
## Statistical Note
The IQR outlier capping method applied boundaries at
$Q1 - 1.5 \times IQR$ (lower) and $Q3 + 1.5 \times IQR$ (upper)
to Transaction Amount, Fraud Amount, Loan Amount, and Income variables.
:::
